name: Phase 11 · Observability
short: The ability to ask any question about your running system and get the answer in minutes — built on logs, metrics, traces, and the discipline to alert only on what matters

detail.what: Observability is the ability to understand what's happening inside your system by examining its external outputs — without deploying new code, attaching a debugger, or SSHing into a machine. It rests on three complementary pillars, each answering a different type of question: (1) Logs — timestamped, structured records of discrete events: 'at 14:32:07.123 UTC, user 42 from IP 203.0.113.5 called POST /api/orders, which returned 201 in 47ms after writing to shard-3.' Logs answer: 'What exactly happened?' They provide the narrative — the forensic evidence for debugging a specific incident or auditing a specific action. (2) Metrics — numerical measurements aggregated over time: 'p99 latency is 220ms, error rate is 0.3%, CPU is at 73%, queue depth is 450, active connections: 1,204.' Metrics answer: 'How is the system performing right now, and how is that trending?' They're cheap to store (pre-aggregated), fast to query, and the foundation of dashboards and alerting. (3) Traces — the complete path of a single request as it traverses multiple services: 'Request abc123 entered the API gateway at t=0, hit auth-service at t=2ms (took 5ms), then order-service at t=8ms (took 30ms, including a 22ms Postgres query and a 3ms Redis lookup), then payment-service at t=40ms (took 150ms including an external Stripe API call).' Traces answer: 'Why is this specific request slow?' They stitch together the per-service view into an end-to-end story. Beyond these three pillars, mature observability includes: profiling (continuous CPU/memory profiling to find hot code paths), SLO tracking (measuring your Service Level Objectives in real-time and managing error budgets), and alerting (the discipline of notifying humans only when action is required). Together, these capabilities let you go from 'something is wrong' to 'this is the root cause and here's the fix' in minutes instead of hours.
detail.why: You cannot fix what you cannot see. Without observability, an outage is detective work: SSH into machines, grep through gigabytes of unstructured logs, form hypotheses, check another machine, realize the problem was in a different service entirely, start over. Engineers spend 4–5 hours per incident on average in poorly observed systems. With proper observability, the same incident plays out differently: PagerDuty fires because the error budget burn rate exceeded 10× normal. The on-call engineer opens a dashboard, sees that the /checkout endpoint's p99 latency spiked from 200ms to 8s starting at 14:22. They click into the trace view, pick a slow request, and see that 7.5s of the 8s was spent in a single Postgres query in the inventory-service. They check the slow query log and find a sequential scan on the orders table — a new feature deployed at 14:20 added a query missing an index. Fix: add the index, latency returns to normal at 14:35. Total incident duration: 13 minutes. The difference between a 13-minute incident and a 5-hour incident is almost entirely observability quality. Beyond incidents, observability pays dividends in: (a) capacity planning — you can see when you're approaching resource ceilings weeks in advance and scale proactively instead of reactively; (b) feature velocity — catching performance regressions in staging or canary before they reach all users; (c) cost optimization — identifying over-provisioned services or inefficient queries that waste money; (d) SLO confidence — knowing your actual reliability numbers so you can make informed decisions about shipping velocity vs. stability.
detail.numbers: Three critical operational thresholds to internalize: (1) SLO-based alerting burn rates (from Google's SRE workbook): alert when error budget is burning at >2× normal rate sustained over 1 hour (slow burn — catches gradual degradation like a growing memory leak or slowly filling disk), OR at >14.4× normal rate over 5 minutes (fast burn — catches sudden failures like a crashed service or broken deploy). These thresholds are carefully calibrated to catch real problems while avoiding false alarms. (2) Trace sampling rates by scale: 100% of traces at small scale (<1K RPS — storage is cheap and every request might matter), 1–10% at medium scale (10K+ RPS — 100% would cost thousands per month in storage), 0.1–1% at large scale (100K+ RPS), <0.1% at hyper scale — but always 100% for error traces and 100% for traces exceeding a latency threshold (e.g., keep all traces where total duration >1s). (3) Metrics cardinality: keep total unique label combinations across all metrics below ~1M for a single Prometheus instance, below ~10M for Prometheus with sharding/federation. Each unique label combination creates a separate time series; Prometheus OOMs at high cardinality. The formula: cardinality = metric_count × product_of_label_value_counts. A single metric with labels {method, endpoint, status_code, user_id} where user_id has 1M values creates 1M+ time series — this will kill your TSDB. Rule: never use unbounded identifiers (user_id, request_id, trace_id) as metric labels. Those belong in logs and traces.
detail.keyInsight: The three pillars serve fundamentally different query patterns. Metrics answer 'what is happening right now and is it normal?' (wide and shallow — aggregate view across all requests). Traces answer 'why is this specific thing slow or broken?' (narrow and deep — detailed view of one request). Logs answer 'what exactly happened in this specific instance?' (forensic detail for incident investigation and audit). Trying to use one pillar for another's job always fails: you can't effectively alert on logs (too noisy, too expensive to aggregate), you can't debug a specific request with metrics (they're pre-aggregated, the individual request is lost), and you can't get system-wide health from traces (too expensive to trace everything, sampling means you miss things). Use all three, connected by a shared correlation ID (trace_id) that lets you jump from a metric anomaly → to a problematic trace → to the detailed logs for that request.

tradeoffs.left: More data means more insight, faster debugging, and better anomaly detection — but observability tooling is expensive: Datadog charges $0.10/GB log ingest, $15/host/month for infra metrics, $31/host/month for APM
tradeoffs.right: Less data means lower cost but slower incident response, missed regressions, and blind spots in system behavior. The cost of an extended outage almost always exceeds the cost of observability tooling.

tradeoffs.left: Full tracing (100% of requests) gives you a complete picture — you can debug any request, find any slow query, catch any intermittent issue. But at 100K RPS, storing every trace costs $10K+/month and the instrumentation overhead can add 1–3% CPU
tradeoffs.right: Sampling (1–10%) dramatically reduces cost and overhead, but you might miss the one request that demonstrates a rare bug. Mitigate with: always trace errors at 100%, always trace requests above a latency threshold, and use tail-based sampling (decide to keep/drop after seeing the full trace)

tradeoffs.left: One platform for everything (Datadog, Grafana Cloud) — unified querying, correlation, and dashboarding. Simpler to operate but expensive at scale and creates vendor lock-in
tradeoffs.right: Best-of-breed per pillar (Prometheus for metrics, Jaeger for traces, ELK for logs) — cheaper, more control, but harder to correlate across pillars and more operational burden

pitfalls.name: Threshold-based alerting hellscape
pitfalls.desc: You alert on 'CPU >80%.' CPU spikes to 85% during a perfectly healthy nightly batch job at 3am — on-call engineer gets paged, investigates for 30 minutes, finds nothing wrong. This happens 4 times a week. The engineer tunes the threshold to 90%. A month later, a real incident causes CPU to hit 88% (because the DB connection pool is exhausted and requests are timing out) — but the alert doesn't fire because the threshold is now 90%. The fundamental problem: infrastructure metrics (CPU, memory, disk) are poor proxies for user impact. CPU can be at 95% and everything's fine (efficient batch processing) or at 50% and everything's broken (deadlock holding all connections). The solution: SLO-based alerting. Define SLOs in terms of user-visible quality: 'p99 latency <200ms for /api/checkout' and 'error rate <0.1% for /api/orders.' Alert when the error budget burn rate exceeds thresholds (2× over 1h for slow burn, 14× over 5min for fast burn). This alerting style fires only when users are actually being impacted, and has been proven at Google scale (documented in the SRE books) to reduce false alerts by 90%+ compared to threshold-based alerting.

pitfalls.name: Logs as primary debugging tool at scale
pitfalls.desc: At 50 services × 1M requests/day, you're generating 500M log lines/day. Grep is dead — searching through 500M lines takes minutes even on fast hardware, and you don't know which of the 50 services to search. 'Find all errors in the last hour' returns 50,000 results. Correlating a request across services requires matching on a request ID buried in free-text log messages. The paradigm shift: use metrics as your primary situational awareness tool (dashboards showing the four golden signals — latency, traffic, error rate, saturation — per service). Use traces as your primary debugging tool ('show me a slow request and where it spent its time'). Logs become evidence — the last resort for forensic detail when metrics and traces have already pointed you at the right service and time window. This hierarchy (metrics → traces → logs) is the Google/Netflix/Uber observability model, and it reduces mean time to diagnosis (MTTD) by 5–10× compared to log-based debugging.

pitfalls.name: High-cardinality labels in metrics
pitfalls.desc: A developer adds 'user_id' as a Prometheus label to track per-user latency. With 10M users, you've just created 10M time series for a single metric. Multiply by 5 labels × 20 metrics and your Prometheus instance needs 100GB+ of memory and falls over. Cardinality — the number of unique label-value combinations — is the enemy of time-series databases. Prometheus is designed for bounded cardinality: labels should be things like {method='GET', endpoint='/api/orders', status='200', region='us-east-1'} — a few hundred combinations total. Unbounded identifiers (user_id, request_id, order_id, trace_id) must NEVER be metric labels. Instead, attach them to logs and traces where storage is per-event rather than per-time-series. If you need per-user metrics, use a dedicated analytics system (ClickHouse, BigQuery) or a high-cardinality observability platform (Honeycomb — purpose-built for this use case).

pitfalls.name: Alert fatigue — the silent killer of incident response
pitfalls.desc: Your team receives 100 alerts per day. 95 are false positives — benign batch jobs, transient network blips, threshold miscalibration. After a month of this, the on-call engineer has learned to ignore alerts. They mute notifications, dismiss without investigating, and treat pages as 'probably nothing.' Then a real incident fires the same alert that's been crying wolf for weeks — but this time it's the database running out of connections and the site is down. The engineer is at dinner and has silenced their phone. The outage lasts 2 hours instead of 15 minutes. Alert fatigue is a recognized failure mode in every high-reliability industry (aviation, medicine, nuclear). The fix: aggressively cull alerts. Every alert must be (1) actionable — there is a specific thing a human should do when it fires, (2) urgent — it can't wait until business hours, and (3) real — it has <5% false positive rate. Delete or auto-resolve everything else. Audit your alert inventory monthly. If an alert hasn't led to a useful investigation in 30 days, delete it.

pitfalls.name: No trace context propagation across service boundaries
pitfalls.desc: You've instrumented each service with OpenTelemetry — each service emits beautiful spans. But when you look at a trace, it stops at the boundary of each service. You can see that the API gateway took 5ms, and separately that the order-service took 150ms, but you can't see them as part of the same request because the trace context (the trace_id and span_id) isn't being propagated between services. The fix: propagate the W3C 'traceparent' header on every HTTP/gRPC call between services. Most OpenTelemetry SDKs do this automatically if you use their HTTP client instrumentation. For Kafka/async: include trace_id in the message headers and link the consumer span as a child of the producer span. Without propagation, you have 50 isolated per-service trace databases, not a distributed tracing system. Test it: make a request and verify you can see the full end-to-end trace through all services in your trace viewer.

pitfalls.name: Observability only in production, not in staging/CI
pitfalls.desc: You catch a performance regression in production after it's affected users for 2 hours. The same regression was present in staging for a week but nobody noticed because staging has no dashboards, no alerting, and no trace analysis. The fix: run the same observability stack in staging as in production (use the same Datadog/Grafana, just tag with environment='staging'). Add performance gates to CI: if a test suite's p99 latency regresses by >20% vs the previous run, fail the build. This shifts detection left — catching regressions before they reach production.

examples.name: Google's Dapper paper (2010) — the origin of distributed tracing
examples.desc: Google published the Dapper paper in 2010, describing how they trace single requests across thousands of microservices in real-time. The key innovations: (1) transparent instrumentation (tracing code injected by the RPC framework, not by individual developers), (2) low-overhead sampling (tracing 1 in 1024 requests, but keeping 100% of slow/error requests), and (3) a trace-aware storage backend. Dapper traced a single Google Search query through ~20 services and ~2000 machines. The paper inspired virtually every distributed tracing system that followed: Zipkin (Twitter, open-sourced 2012), Jaeger (Uber, open-sourced 2017), and OpenTelemetry (CNCF, the current standard). The practical lesson: distributed tracing is not optional in a microservices architecture — it's the only way to answer 'why is this request slow?' when a request touches 10 services. Without it, debugging is guesswork.

examples.name: Honeycomb's high-cardinality observability — beyond the three pillars
examples.desc: Honeycomb (founded by Charity Majors, former Parse/Facebook infrastructure engineer) was built around the insight that the most interesting debugging questions involve high-cardinality data: 'Why are requests from user 47 on iOS 16 in Brazil slow?' This query requires filtering on user_id (10M values), device_OS (20 values), and country (200 values) — impossible with Prometheus-style pre-aggregated metrics. Honeycomb stores structured events (similar to logs but with typed fields) in a columnar database and computes aggregations at query time. The tradeoff: higher storage and query cost than pre-aggregated metrics, but the ability to ask arbitrary questions without predicting them ahead of time. This approach — sometimes called 'observability 2.0' — is especially powerful for debugging intermittent issues that only affect specific combinations of dimensions. If Prometheus answers 'what is the p99 latency?', Honeycomb answers 'what is the p99 latency for users on slow mobile connections in Southeast Asia using the new checkout flow?' — a question you'd never think to create a Prometheus metric for.

examples.name: Slack's observability evolution — from logs to traces to SLOs
examples.desc: Slack's observability journey is well-documented: they started with logs only (ELK stack), then added metrics (StatsD → Graphite → Prometheus), then distributed tracing (Jaeger), then SLO-based alerting. The key transition was from threshold alerts ('CPU >80%') to SLO alerts ('error budget for message delivery is burning at 5× normal rate'). This single change reduced their on-call page volume by ~80% while improving mean-time-to-detection. Their architecture: OpenTelemetry for instrumentation → Kafka for telemetry transport → ClickHouse for trace storage → Grafana for dashboards → custom SLO alerting engine. The lesson: observability is an evolutionary journey, not a one-time installation. Start with structured logs and basic metrics, add tracing when you have >5 services, and graduate to SLO-based alerting when alert volume becomes unmanageable.

levelUp.trigger: Any production incident takes >1 hour to diagnose, or you find yourself SSHing into machines and grepping logs to debug, or you have no idea what your actual error rate or latency percentiles are
levelUp.action: Step 1: Add structured logging (JSON format, not free text) with consistent fields: timestamp, level, service, trace_id, user_id, endpoint, duration_ms, status_code. Use a structured logger: Winston (Node.js), structlog (Python), Logrus/Zap (Go), Log4j2 JSON layout (Java). Step 2: Add error tracking — Sentry (free for 5K events/month, $26/month for 50K) captures every unhandled exception with full stack trace, breadcrumbs, and user context. This alone cuts debugging time in half. Step 3: Add uptime monitoring — UptimeRobot (free for 50 monitors) or Better Stack ($24/month) pings your endpoints every 60 seconds and alerts on downtime via Slack/SMS/PagerDuty. Step 4: Add basic APM — NewRelic (free for 100GB/month ingest) or Datadog free tier (5 hosts) gives you per-request breakdown, slow query identification, and dependency mapping. Step 5: Create three dashboards: (a) system health (CPU, memory, disk, network per host), (b) application health (RPS, error rate, p50/p95/p99 latency per endpoint), (c) business metrics (signups, orders, revenue). This entire stack takes 1–2 days to set up and transforms your debugging capability.

levelUp.trigger: You have >10 services and correlating logs across them manually is painful, or your threshold-based alerts fire >5 false positives per week, or you can't answer 'why was this specific request slow?' without 30 minutes of investigation
levelUp.action: Step 1: Implement distributed tracing with OpenTelemetry (the CNCF standard — vendor-neutral, wide language support). Instrument each service with the OpenTelemetry SDK for your language. Ensure trace context (W3C traceparent header) propagates on every inter-service call. Deploy a trace backend: Grafana Tempo (open source, pairs with Grafana, uses object storage so cost scales with volume), Jaeger (open source, battle-tested at Uber), or Datadog APM ($31/host/month, managed, excellent UI). Step 2: Centralize logs — ship structured logs from all services to one platform. ELK Stack (self-hosted: Elasticsearch + Logstash + Kibana, free but operationally heavy — Elasticsearch clusters are notoriously difficult to tune and operate) vs Datadog Logs ($0.10/GB ingest + $0.06/GB/month retention, managed, excellent correlation with APM) vs Grafana Loki (open source, designed to be like Prometheus but for logs — indexes only labels, not content, making it 10× cheaper to operate than Elasticsearch). Recommendation: Grafana Loki for cost-conscious teams, Datadog for teams that value developer experience over cost. Step 3: Build per-service dashboards showing the four golden signals (from Google's SRE book): latency (p50, p95, p99), traffic (RPS), error rate (% 5xx), saturation (CPU, memory, connection pool usage). Step 4: Migrate from threshold-based to SLO-based alerting. Define SLOs for your top 5 user-facing endpoints. Implement error budget burn rate alerting using Sloth (open source SLO tool that generates Prometheus recording rules) or Datadog SLOs. Delete or silence all threshold-based alerts that have fired >3 false positives in the last month. Step 5: Create runbook automation — for each alert, write a runbook document that describes: what the alert means, what to check first, likely root causes, and how to mitigate. Link the runbook directly in the alert notification.

levelUp.trigger: Alert volume is unmanageable (>20 actionable alerts/week across all services), observability costs exceed $50K/month, or you need to detect anomalies that humans can't spot by watching dashboards
levelUp.action: Step 1: Implement ML-based anomaly detection. Rather than static thresholds or even burn-rate alerts, use statistical models that learn each metric's normal behavior and alert when current behavior deviates significantly. Options: Datadog's built-in anomaly detection (Bayesian model, works out of the box), Amazon CloudWatch Anomaly Detection, or custom models using Prophet (Facebook's time-series forecasting) / Holt-Winters on your Prometheus data. These catch slow-drift anomalies (gradually increasing latency over 3 days) that humans and static alerts miss. Step 2: Build automated incident remediation for known failure modes. If 'high error rate on service-X due to unhealthy pods' fires 5 times a month with the same fix (restart pods), automate it: the alert triggers a runbook bot (PagerDuty Runbook Automation, Rundeck, or custom Lambda/CronJob) that executes the fix, verifies recovery, and only pages a human if automated remediation fails. Target: 30% of pages handled automatically within 6 months. Step 3: Move to tail-based trace sampling — instead of randomly sampling 1% of traces at the start (head-based, which means you discard 99% of traces before you know if they're interesting), use a trace collector (OpenTelemetry Collector with tail-sampling processor) that buffers traces for 30 seconds, then decides to keep traces that have errors, exceed latency thresholds, or match specific criteria. This gives you 100% capture of interesting traces at the storage cost of 1–5% sampling. Step 4: Implement SLO budgets enforced in CI/CD — if a service has consumed >80% of its monthly error budget, block deploys to that service unless the deploy is a fix. This creates natural tension between velocity and reliability, managed by the error budget rather than by organizational politics. Step 5: Real-time capacity heatmaps — build dashboards that show per-service, per-region resource utilization as a heatmap with forecasted time-to-exhaustion. This turns capacity planning from a quarterly spreadsheet exercise into a continuously updated live view.

defined_terms.Golden signals: The four key metrics Google's SRE team monitors for every service: latency (how long requests take), traffic (how many requests per second), error rate (what percentage of requests fail), and saturation (how 'full' the service is — CPU, memory, queue depth). If you can only have four dashboards, build these four.
defined_terms.Error budget: The allowed amount of unreliability implied by your SLO. If your SLO is 99.9%, your error budget is 0.1% of requests per month. When the budget is exhausted, the team should freeze feature deploys and focus on reliability. Error budgets make the reliability-vs-velocity tradeoff explicit and quantitative.
defined_terms.Trace sampling: The practice of recording only a fraction of request traces to reduce storage cost and overhead. Head-based sampling decides at request start (random); tail-based sampling decides after the request completes (can keep errors and slow requests at 100%). Tail-based is strictly better but requires a buffering collector.
defined_terms.Cardinality: The number of unique values a label/dimension can take. Low cardinality: {region} with 5 values. High cardinality: {user_id} with 10M values. Time-series databases (Prometheus, InfluxDB) struggle with high cardinality because each unique combination is a separate time series consuming memory. Event databases (Honeycomb, ClickHouse) handle high cardinality well because they store individual events and aggregate at query time.
defined_terms.SLO-based alerting: Instead of alerting on infrastructure thresholds (CPU >80%), alert when the rate of SLO violation (error budget consumption) exceeds a threshold. This aligns alerts with user impact rather than machine state.

small.range: Basic logging + error tracking + uptime ping
small.rec: The minimum viable observability stack for any production system: (1) Structured JSON logs with consistent fields across all endpoints — this costs nothing and transforms debugging. Don't log raw request bodies (PII leak risk); log: timestamp, level, service, endpoint, method, status_code, duration_ms, trace_id, user_id. (2) Error tracking with Sentry — captures every unhandled exception with full stack trace, request context, and user information. Sentry's free tier (5K events/month) is sufficient for most small applications. (3) Uptime monitoring with UptimeRobot (free for 50 monitors) — pings your key endpoints every 60 seconds and alerts via email/Slack/SMS if they go down. (4) Application performance monitoring (APM) — NewRelic's free tier (100GB/month data ingest) or Datadog's free tier (up to 5 hosts) gives you automatic per-request breakdown, slow query identification, and a dependency map. Log retention: 7 days is sufficient at this scale (if you need older logs, push to S3 for cold storage at $0.023/GB/month). Technology comparison: Sentry ($0–$26/month, best error tracking UX, captures frontend + backend, source maps for JS) vs Bugsnag (similar features, $59/month starting, slightly better mobile support) vs Rollbar ($0–$49/month, more analytics-oriented). UptimeRobot (free, simple, 50 monitors) vs Better Stack ($24/month, prettier dashboards, includes status page) vs Pingdom ($10/month, Atlassian-owned, more features but expensive for what you get). For APM at small scale: NewRelic (generous free tier, good for getting started) vs Datadog (better UI/UX, more expensive, better at scale) vs Grafana Cloud (free tier with 50GB metrics + 50GB logs + 50GB traces, good if you prefer open-source tooling).

medium.range: Metrics + centralized structured logs + SLO-aware alerts
medium.rec: At this scale, you need to upgrade from 'can I see errors?' to 'do I have real-time visibility into system health and can I be alerted when users are impacted?' (1) Metrics with Prometheus + Grafana (both open source): Prometheus scrapes metrics from your services every 15 seconds and stores them in a time-series database; Grafana visualizes them in dashboards. The combination is the de facto standard for Kubernetes-native metrics and is free to self-host. Alternatively: Datadog ($15/host/month for infrastructure + $31/host/month for APM) provides metrics, traces, and logs in one platform — more expensive but dramatically less operational burden than self-hosting Prometheus + Grafana + Jaeger + ELK. (2) Centralized structured logs: ship logs from all services to one searchable store. Grafana Loki (open source, designed to be 'Prometheus but for logs' — indexes only labels, not full text, making it 10× cheaper to operate than Elasticsearch; pairs beautifully with Grafana) vs ELK Stack (Elasticsearch + Logstash + Kibana — powerful full-text search but operationally demanding; Elasticsearch clusters are among the hardest infrastructure to run reliably) vs Datadog Logs ($0.10/GB ingest, managed). Recommendation: Loki if self-hosting, Datadog if you can afford managed. (3) PagerDuty ($21/user/month) or OpsGenie ($9/user/month) for on-call alerting — integrates with Prometheus/Datadog, handles escalation, scheduling, and incident tracking. (4) Basic distributed tracing: Jaeger (open source, deployed easily with the all-in-one Docker image for evaluation) — even basic tracing that connects 2–3 services dramatically improves debugging. (5) Custom business metrics — instrument your code to track business KPIs alongside system metrics: conversion rate, checkout completion, revenue per minute, signup rate. These are the metrics that matter most and the ones that are hardest to reconstruct later. Log retention: 30 days for searchable logs, 90 days in cold storage (S3).

large.range: Distributed tracing + SLO-based alerting + service dependency maps + runbook automation
large.rec: At this scale (10+ services, hundreds of thousands of daily active users), observability transitions from 'a tool' to 'a core engineering discipline.' (1) Distributed tracing on every request: deploy OpenTelemetry SDK in every service. Use auto-instrumentation where available (Java agent, Python auto-instrumentor, Node.js auto-instrumentation) to capture HTTP, gRPC, database, and cache calls with zero code changes. Backend: Grafana Tempo (open source, stores traces in object storage S3/GCS — cost-effective at scale, ~$0.01/GB/month for trace storage) vs Jaeger (open source, more mature UI, but uses Elasticsearch or Cassandra backend which is more expensive to operate) vs Datadog APM ($31/host/month, best-in-class UI, automatic service dependency maps, AI-powered root cause analysis) vs Honeycomb ($100+/month, the best tool for high-cardinality debugging — if your hardest bugs are 'why is this specific user slow?', Honeycomb is unmatched). (2) SLO-based alerting: define SLOs for your top 10 user-facing operations. Use Sloth (open source, generates Prometheus recording rules for SLO tracking and multi-window burn rate alerts) or Datadog SLOs (built-in). Alert on error budget burn rate, not on thresholds. This single change typically reduces alert volume by 60–90%. (3) Service dependency maps: auto-generated from trace data — Datadog and Grafana both render these. Essential for understanding blast radius ('if service X goes down, what's affected?') and for on-call engineers who didn't build the system. (4) Automated incident correlation: when 5 alerts fire simultaneously, group them into one incident and identify the root cause. PagerDuty AIOps ($49/user/month) and Datadog Watchdog do this. (5) Runbook automation: for every alert, maintain a linked runbook. For common, well-understood incidents (disk full, pod crash loop, certificate expiring), automate the remediation via PagerDuty Runbook Automation or Rundeck (open source). Log retention: 30 days hot (searchable), 1 year cold (S3/GCS), with compliance-required logs (auth, PII access) retained per regulation.

hyper.range: Full observability platform — custom TSDB, ML anomaly detection, automated remediation, SLO budgets in CI/CD
hyper.rec: At hyper scale (500+ services, millions of daily active users, >$100K/month observability budget), off-the-shelf observability platforms either can't handle the data volume or become prohibitively expensive. Most hyper-scale companies build custom observability platforms composed of open-source foundations + custom glue + ML layers. (1) Custom or federated metrics: Prometheus federation (one Prometheus per cluster, a global Prometheus scraping the federators) or Thanos/Cortex/Mimir (long-term storage for Prometheus metrics in object storage — Mimir, from Grafana Labs, is the most actively developed). For truly massive metrics (>100M active time series), companies like Uber (M3), Netflix (Atlas), and Facebook (Gorilla/ODS) build custom TSDBs. (2) Trace sampling: move from head-based to tail-based sampling via OpenTelemetry Collector with tail-sampling processor. Keep 100% of error traces and high-latency traces; sample 0.1% of successful traces. Store in Tempo or a custom ClickHouse-based trace store. (3) ML-based anomaly detection: deploy statistical models (Prophet, Holt-Winters, or custom neural models) on key metrics to detect anomalies that static rules miss — gradual latency increases, subtle shifts in error patterns, unusual traffic distributions. Datadog's built-in anomaly detection is a good starting point; at extreme scale, custom models trained on your specific patterns outperform generic solutions. (4) Automated incident remediation: for known, well-characterized failure modes (roughly 30–50% of all incidents are repeated patterns), build automated remediation bots. When the alert fires, the bot executes the runbook (restart pods, scale up, failover traffic, rollback deploy), verifies recovery, and only pages a human if automated fix fails. Target: reduce human-handled incidents by 30–50% within a year. (5) SLO budgets enforced in CI/CD pipeline: if a service has consumed >80% of its monthly error budget, non-critical deploys to that service are blocked by the CI/CD system. Only bug fixes and reliability improvements are allowed through. This operationalizes the Google SRE principle of 'when the error budget is exhausted, stop shipping features and focus on reliability.' (6) Real-time capacity heatmaps: per-service, per-region visualization of resource utilization with ML-predicted time-to-exhaustion. Replaces the quarterly capacity planning spreadsheet with a continuous, live feed. Technology choices at this tier: Grafana Mimir for metrics (open source, multi-tenant, object storage backend, handles billions of active series), ClickHouse for traces and high-cardinality analytics (open source, columnar, fast aggregation, used by Uber, Cloudflare, GitLab), Grafana for dashboards and alerting (open source, the lingua franca of observability visualization), OpenTelemetry for instrumentation (vendor-neutral, avoid lock-in), and custom Python/Go services for ML anomaly detection and automated remediation.
